text.title: Storybook as Source of Truth Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that convert Storybook from documentation site to design-system Source of Truth. Each rule is enforced in CI or PR review; violation blocks merge, not warns.

rule.statement: Every component MUST have one story per distinct visual state: default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error, empty, success, dark theme. States that are unreachable for the component (e.g. button has no "empty") MUST be documented as N/A in the component README. Adding a new state to the component implementation without adding the matching story is a CI failure.

rationale.source: Storybook authoring conventions; visual-regression coverage requirements

rule.rationale: A SoT must cover every state the component can render. Skipping states (especially focus / error / dark theme) leads to drift between code and the design system that re-emerges as a11y or visual regressions weeks later. CI enforcement makes the discipline self-sustaining.

rule.statement: The `@storybook/addon-a11y` MUST run in CI on every PR that touches `src/components/`, and any new rule violation (vs. main baseline) MUST block merge. Existing baseline violations are tracked in a CHANGELOG-style file and burned down sprint-by-sprint; net new violations are forbidden.

rationale.source: WCAG 2.2 compliance trends; cost of post-hoc a11y remediation

rule.rationale: A11y violations are 10-50x cheaper to fix at component-write time than post-launch. The addon catches the cheap-to-fix tier (contrast, ARIA, focus order); without a CI gate the team learns to ignore yellow icons. Treating new violations as PR-blocking aligns incentives.

rule.statement: Component styles inside the design system package MUST consume design tokens (CSS custom properties or token JS imports). Hard-coded colours (`#ffffff`), spacing values (`16px`), or font sizes are FORBIDDEN. A lint rule (Stylelint `declaration-property-value-allowed-list` or equivalent) MUST enforce this in CI.

rationale.source: Design tokens W3C draft; tokens-as-API discipline

rule.rationale: Tokens are the contract between design and code. Hard-coded values escape that contract — a brand refresh or theme addition requires hunting them down. Lint enforcement converts the discipline from review-time hope to compile-time guarantee.

rule.statement: Figma component libraries MUST be generated from Storybook (e.g. Storybook Figma plugin, Specify, design-tokens sync), NOT the other way around. Designers consume the generated library; they MUST NOT redraw components in Figma as the canonical reference. Sync runs on every Storybook release.
rule.rationale: When Figma is canonical, designers ship visual specs that engineering re-implements with subtle drift — the same pattern that motivates "Storybook as SoT" in the first place. Generating Figma from code closes the loop: the design library cannot drift from the implementation because it IS the implementation's rendered output.

rule.statement: When product code uses a component-like pattern outside the design system (one-off styling, inline overrides, "just this once" CSS), the PR review MUST add a comment linking to the relevant Storybook story or open a follow-up ticket to add the component. Inline approvals of "we'll fix it later" without a tracked ticket are FORBIDDEN.
rule.rationale: One-off deviations are how design systems erode. The PR-comment-or-ticket rule converts each deviation into a tracked decision instead of a silent escape hatch. Over a quarter, this is the single biggest determinant of whether the system stays canonical.

example.title: Story-per-state coverage for a Button component
example.note: r1 + r2 satisfied. The a11y addon parameter blocks merge on any new violation. Each visual state is reachable by URL for visual regression and Figma generation.

example.title: Storybook treated as a one-off demo site
example.note: r1, r2, r3 all violated. Only one state covered; no a11y addon; hard-coded colour and spacing escape the token contract. Storybook drifts within weeks.
